::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Download Notebook
[Download as Jupyter Notebook](notebooks/beam_model_introduction.ipynb)
:::

## Learning Objectives

After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

- Identify the physical parameters (moduli, Poisson ratios, geometry) in a composite cantilever beam
- Understand the model hierarchy from 2D plane-stress elasticity to 1D Euler-Bernoulli beam theory
- Formulate tip deflection as a scalar Quantity of Interest (QoI) for Uncertainty Quantification (UQ)

## Prerequisites

Complete [UQ Problem Framing](uq_problem_framing.qmd) before this tutorial.

## The Physical Setup

Composite beams with internal holes appear in aerospace, civil, and marine structures. Material properties vary due to manufacturing tolerances, and the question changes from "what is the stress field?" to "how confident are we in the answer?"

This cantilever beam serves as the running example for the UQ workshops. @fig-beam-geometry shows the geometry: a rectangular domain with three material layers, five circular holes in the core, clamped at the left edge, and a linearly increasing traction on the top surface.

::: {#cell-fig-beam-geometry .cell execution_count=1}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Composite cantilever beam: three material layers with five circular holes. Clamped at the left edge, linearly increasing traction on the top surface.](beam_model_introduction_files/figure-html/fig-beam-geometry-output-1.png){#fig-beam-geometry width=886 height=374}
:::
:::


The beam has three subdomains: stiff skins (top and bottom, $E_1 > E_2$) and a compliant core with holes. The four uncertain material parameters -- $E_1$, $\nu_1$, $E_2$, $\nu_2$ -- are the inputs we vary throughout the UQ workshops.

## Governing Equations

The stress-strain relationship in each layer follows Hooke's law:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \varepsilon_{kk} \, \delta_{ij} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}
$$ {#eq-hooke}

where the Lame parameters $\lambda$ and $\mu$ depend on the layer's Young's modulus $E_i$ and Poisson ratio $\nu_i$.

Equilibrium and boundary conditions define the boundary value problem:

$$
\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} = \vc{0} \;\text{in}\; \Omega, \qquad
\vc{u} = \vc{0} \;\text{on}\; \Gamma_D, \qquad
\boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \vc{n} = \vc{t} \;\text{on}\; \Gamma_N
$$ {#eq-strong-form}

where $\vc{t} = (0,\, -q_0 x/L)^T$ is the linearly increasing traction on the top surface.

The scalar QoI is the vertical displacement at the beam tip:

$$
\delta_{\text{tip}} = u_y(L,\, H/2)
$$ {#eq-qoi}

## Reference Solution

@fig-reference-solution shows the deformed shape and displacement magnitude when the beam is solved at fixed (mean) material properties. The deformation is magnified for visualization; the actual displacements are small relative to the beam dimensions.

::: {#cell-fig-reference-solution .cell execution_count=2}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Von Mises stress on the deformed mesh (magnified) with undeformed outline. Stress concentrations appear at the holes near the clamped end.](beam_model_introduction_files/figure-html/fig-reference-solution-output-1.png){#fig-reference-solution width=878 height=316}
:::
:::


## Model Hierarchy: 1D vs 2D

A 1D Euler-Bernoulli beam provides a fast approximation of the tip deflection. The beam equation $EI\,w''''(x) = q(x)$ governs the transverse (vertical) deflection $w(x)$ of the beam centerline, where $q(x)$ is the distributed load per unit length (negative downward). @fig-model-hierarchy compares the 1D deflection curve with the full 2D displacement field. Both models predict similar tip deflection, but the 2D model captures stress concentrations around the holes that the 1D model cannot represent.

::: {#cell-fig-model-hierarchy .cell execution_count=3}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Model hierarchy. Left: 1D beam deflection curve. Right: 2D von Mises stress on deformed mesh with undeformed outline. Both predict similar tip deflection.](beam_model_introduction_files/figure-html/fig-model-hierarchy-output-1.png){#fig-model-hierarchy width=1103 height=324}
:::
:::


## Loading the Benchmark

The PyApprox benchmark wraps the full 2D FEM solve into a callable function. The uncertain inputs are Karhunen-Loeve Expansion (KLE) coefficients that parameterize spatially varying Young's modulus fields over each subdomain.

::: {#1eb21396 .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.typing.benchmarks.instances.pde import cantilever_beam_2d_linear
from pyapprox.typing.benchmarks.instances.pde.cantilever_beam import MESH_PATHS

bkd = NumpyBkd()
benchmark = cantilever_beam_2d_linear(bkd, mesh_path=MESH_PATHS[2])
model = benchmark.function()
print(f"Input:  {model.nvars()} KLE coefficients (standard normal)")
print(f"Output: {model.nqoi()} QoIs (tip deflection, total von Mises stress)")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Input:  6 KLE coefficients (standard normal)
Output: 2 QoIs (tip deflection, total von Mises stress)
```
:::
:::


## Key Takeaways

- The composite cantilever beam has uncertain material parameters ($E_1, \nu_1, E_2, \nu_2$) across three layers
- The 2D plane-stress model captures stress concentrations at holes; the 1D beam provides a fast approximation
- Tip deflection $\delta_{\text{tip}}$ is the scalar QoI for all subsequent UQ tutorials
- The benchmark API wraps the full FEM solve into a callable function for Monte Carlo sampling

## Exercises

1. How would increasing the number of holes affect the tip deflection? Which model (1D or 2D) would better capture this effect?

2. If the skin stiffness $E_1$ doubles while the core stiffness $E_2$ stays fixed, predict qualitatively how $\delta_{\text{tip}}$ changes and why.

## Next Steps

- [Discretization Error](discretization_error_concept.qmd) -- Mesh refinement and numerical accuracy
- [Model Misspecification](model_misspecification_concept.qmd) -- Linear vs nonlinear constitutive models
